name: Boss
description: The world's most popular pedal brand. Known for bulletproof build quality and consistent tone.
intro: Since 1976, Boss has been the most trusted name in guitar effects. Their compact, indestructible pedals have appeared on more pedalboards — from bedroom players to stadium headliners — than any other brand in history.

highlights.title: Built to Last
highlights.text: Die-cast zinc enclosures survive decades of touring. Boss pedals from the 1980s still work perfectly today.

highlights.title: Unbeatable Value
highlights.text: Professional-quality effects at accessible prices. The DS-1 has been used on countless hit records.

highlights.title: Proven by Legends
highlights.text: Kurt Cobain, Steve Vai, John Frusciante, and thousands of touring pros trust Boss on stage every night.

history.title: Boss Brand History
paragraphs: Boss was founded in 1976 as a division of Roland Corporation in Japan. Their first pedal, the CE-1 Chorus Ensemble, set the standard for chorus effects and is still sought after today.
The iconic compact pedal format — the same size used today — was introduced in 1977 with the OD-1 OverDrive. This design became the template for the entire industry.

Through the 1980s and 90s, Boss released some of the most influential pedals ever made: the DS-1 Distortion (1978), the DD-2 Digital Delay (1983, the world's first digital delay pedal), and the MT-2 Metal Zone (1991).

Today Boss continues to innovate with digital multi-effects, the Waza Craft premium line, and modern takes on their classic designs — while maintaining the same bulletproof build quality that made them famous.

faqs.a: Most Boss pedals use buffered bypass, not true bypass. This is actually a feature — Boss buffers are high quality and help maintain signal integrity over long cable runs.

faqs.q: What is the best Boss pedal for beginners?
faqs.a: The Boss DS-1 and SD-1 are both excellent starting points. They're affordable, durable, and cover the most essential gain tones.

faqs.q: How long do Boss pedals last?
faqs.a: Boss pedals are legendary for durability. Many players use pedals from the 1980s that still work perfectly. The die-cast zinc enclosure handles decades of gigging.

faqs.q: Do Boss pedals need a power supply?
faqs.a: Boss pedals run on 9V DC (center negative) or a 9V battery. Compatible with any standard guitar pedal power supply.

name: Ibanez
description: Home of the legendary Tube Screamer, Ibanez makes some of the most iconic overdrive pedals ever.

name: Electro-Harmonix
description: NYC-based maker of the Big Muff Pi and countless other legendary effects pedals.

name: MXR
description: Classic American pedal brand known for simple, great-sounding effects in compact enclosures.

name: Behringer
description: Budget-friendly pedals that clone classic circuits at a fraction of the price.
intro: Behringer has made professional-grade effects accessible to every guitarist. Their pedals deliver classic tones at a fraction of the cost, making them the go-to choice for beginners, students, and budget-conscious players worldwide.

highlights.title: Lowest Prices
highlights.text: Most Behringer pedals cost under $30. You can build a complete pedalboard for less than the price of a single boutique pedal.

highlights.title: Classic Circuits
highlights.text: Many Behringer pedals are based on legendary circuits — the TO800 mirrors the Tube Screamer, the SF300 channels the Big Muff.

highlights.title: True Bypass
highlights.text: Most Behringer pedals feature true bypass switching, preserving your tone when the effect is disengaged.

items.name: TO800 Vintage Tube Overdrive
items.text: A Tube Screamer-inspired overdrive for under $25. Warm, mid-focused tone at a fraction of the cost.

items.name: SF300 Super Fuzz
items.text: Three fuzz modes covering everything from vintage Big Muff tones to modern fuzz textures.

items.name: DR600 Digital Reverb
items.text: Six reverb modes including spring, hall, and plate. Excellent value for ambient players.

items.name: CS400 Compressor/Sustainer
items.text: Studio-style compression at a beginner-friendly price. Great for country and clean tones.

faqs.a: Yes — Behringer pedals are surprisingly capable for the price. They are ideal for beginners, practice rigs, and backup boards. Many are based on classic circuits that have been proven for decades.

faqs.q: How do Behringer pedals compare to Boss?
faqs.a: Boss pedals have better build quality and reliability for gigging. Behringer pedals cost significantly less and are great for home use, practice, and experimenting with new effects.

faqs.q: Do Behringer pedals use true bypass?
faqs.a: Most Behringer pedals use true bypass switching, which is actually better than many pricier alternatives for preserving your tone when the effect is off.

faqs.q: Are Behringer pedals good for beginners?
faqs.a: Absolutely. Behringer pedals let beginners explore a wide range of effects without a large investment. Once you know what you like, you can upgrade to premium versions.

name: Joyo
description: Affordable pedals with surprisingly good tone. Great for beginners building their first board.

name: TC Electronic
description: Known for pristine digital effects and the innovative TonePrint technology.

name: Walrus Audio
description: Boutique pedal maker from Oklahoma known for creative designs and premium tone.

name: JHS Pedals
description: Boutique builder known for YouTube education and meticulously crafted effects.
intro: JHS Pedals is a Kansas City boutique builder founded by Josh Scott in 2007. Beyond meticulously crafted drives, fuzzes, and modulation, JHS is known to a generation of players through 'The JHS Show' — a YouTube channel that has done more to teach guitarists about pedal history than any brand before it.

highlights.title: Education First
highlights.text: 'The JHS Show' turned pedal history into must-watch YouTube, earning a level of trust few brands enjoy.

highlights.title: Boutique Craft
highlights.text: Designed and built in Kansas City, JHS pedals are obsessively voiced for feel and dynamic response.

highlights.title: Transparent Drive Pioneers
highlights.text: The Morning Glory helped define the 'transparent overdrive' category that so many players now chase.

history.title: JHS Pedals — From Repairs to Icon
paragraphs: JHS began with Josh Scott modifying and repairing pedals before launching his own designs in 2007. Early modded Tube Screamers and Boss circuits built a reputation for taste and quality that the brand still trades on today.
The Morning Glory — a low-gain, transparent overdrive — became the pedal that put JHS on the map, and remains the brand's signature drive across multiple revisions.
JHS expanded the catalogue with crowd favourites like the Bonsai (nine classic screamer circuits in one box), the Muffuletta (a multi-era Big Muff tribute), and the Colour Box preamp.
More than gear, 'The JHS Show' made the brand a household name among guitarists, demystifying pedal history and circuit lineage for millions of viewers.

faqs.q: What is JHS Pedals known for?
faqs.a: JHS is known for transparent overdrives like the Morning Glory, multi-circuit boxes like the Bonsai, and 'The JHS Show' — a hugely popular YouTube channel about pedal history and tone.
JHS pedals are designed and built in Kansas City, Missouri, USA, where founder Josh Scott started the company in 2007.

faqs.q: What is the most popular JHS pedal?
faqs.a: The Morning Glory transparent overdrive is the brand's signature pedal. The Bonsai, which packs nine Tube Screamer-style circuits into one enclosure, is another long-running favourite.

faqs.q: Are JHS pedals worth the price?
faqs.a: JHS sits in the boutique tier, so they cost more than mass-market pedals. Players pay for careful circuit voicing, build quality, and a brand with deep knowledge of pedal history.

name: Strymon
description: Premium digital effects with studio-quality algorithms. The gold standard for delay and reverb.

name: ProCo
description: Makers of the legendary RAT distortion pedal since 1978.

name: Dunlop
description: Home of the Cry Baby wah and Fuzz Face. Essential effects for every guitarist.

name: EarthQuaker Devices
description: Akron, Ohio boutique maker known for creative, hand-built effects pedals.
